JavaScript.title: What is JavaScript?
JavaScript.content: JavaScript is a high-level, interpreted programming language primarily used to create dynamic and interactive effects on websites. It allows developers to control multimedia, animate images, validate

JavaScript.title: Where JavaScript Runs
JavaScript.content: JavaScript runs inside web browsers such as Chrome, Firefox, and Edge, allowing code to execute directly on the user's device. It can also run outside the browser using environments like Node.js, enabling server-side development and backend programming.

JavaScript.title: JavaScript Syntax
JavaScript.content: Syntax refers to the set of rules that define how JavaScript code must be written and structured. Correct syntax ensures that the program can be understood and executed by the JavaScript engine without errors

JavaScript.title: Variables
JavaScript.content: Variables are containers used to store data values such as numbers, text, or objects. In JavaScript,

JavaScript.title: let, var, const
JavaScript.content: These are keywords used to declare variables. var is function-scoped, let is block-scoped, and const is also block-scoped but cannot be reassigned after initialization, making it useful for constants.

JavaScript.title: Data Types
JavaScript.content: Data types define the kind of value a variable can hold. Common JavaScript data types include string, number, boolean, null, undefined, object, and symbol.

JavaScript.title: Strings
JavaScript.content: Strings are used to represent text and are enclosed in single quotes, double quotes, or backticks. They are commonly used for displaying messages and storing textual data.

JavaScript.title: Numbers
JavaScript.content: Numbers in JavaScript represent both integers and floating-point values. JavaScript does not differentiate between different numeric types like some other languages.

JavaScript.title: Boolean
JavaScript.content: A boolean represents one of two values: true or false. It is commonly used in decision-making and conditional statements.

JavaScript.title: Operators
JavaScript.content: Operators are symbols used to perform operations on variables and values, such as addition, subtraction, comparison, and logical operations.

JavaScript.title: Assignment Operator
JavaScript.content: The assignment operator (=) is used to assign values to variables. It stores the value on the right side into the variable on the left.

JavaScript.title: Comparison Operators
JavaScript.content: These operators compare two values and return a boolean result. Examples include ==, ===, !=, and >.

JavaScript.title: if Statement
JavaScript.content: The if statement allows code execution based on a condition. If the condition is true, the code block inside it will run.

JavaScript.title: else Statement
JavaScript.content: The else statement runs when the condition in the if statement is false, providing an alternative path of

JavaScript.title: Functions
JavaScript.content: Functions are reusable blocks of code designed to perform a specific task. They help organize code and reduce repetition.

JavaScript.title: Parameters
JavaScript.content: Parameters are inputs passed into functions that allow them to process data and return results based on the input provided.

JavaScript.title: Return Statement
JavaScript.content: The return statement sends a value back from a function to where it was called, allowing the result to be reused.

JavaScript.title: Arrays
JavaScript.content: Arrays are used to store multiple values in a single variable. Each value is indexed, starting from zero.

JavaScript.title: Objects
JavaScript.content: Objects store data in key-value pairs, allowing more structured and meaningful data representation compared to arrays.

JavaScript.title: Loops
JavaScript.content: Loops allow repeated execution of code blocks until a condition is met, making repetitive tasks more efficient and automated.

JavaScript.title: for Loop
JavaScript.content: A for loop is used when you know how many times you want a block of code to run. It consists of an initialization, condition, and increment/decrement, making it ideal for counting iterations.

JavaScript.title: while Loop
JavaScript.content: A while loop executes a block of code as long as a specified condition remains true. It is useful when the number of iterations is not known beforehand.

JavaScript.title: do...while Loop
JavaScript.content: A do...while loop ensures that a block of code runs at least once before checking the condition, making it different from other loops.

JavaScript.title: Break Statement
JavaScript.content: The break statement is used to exit a loop or switch statement immediately, even if the condition is still

JavaScript.title: Continue Statement
JavaScript.content: The continue statement skips the current iteration of a loop and proceeds to the next one without stopping the loop entirely.

JavaScript.title: Switch Statement
JavaScript.content: A switch statement allows multiple conditions to be checked in a cleaner way than multiple if-else statements, especially when comparing the same variable.

JavaScript.title: Events
JavaScript.content: Events are actions that happen in the browser, such as clicks, key presses, or mouse movements, which JavaScript can respond to.

JavaScript.title: Event Listeners
JavaScript.content: Event listeners are used to detect and respond to events. They allow you to attach a function that runs when a specific event occurs.

JavaScript.title: DOM (Document Object Model)
JavaScript.content: The DOM is a programming interface that represents an HTML document as a tree structure, allowing JavaScript to access and manipulate elements dynamically.

JavaScript.title: Selecting Elements
JavaScript.content: JavaScript can select HTML elements using methods like getElementById, querySelector, and others to modify or interact with them.

JavaScript.title: Changing Content
JavaScript.content: JavaScript can modify the content of HTML elements using properties like innerHTML or textContent.

JavaScript.title: Changing Styles
JavaScript.content: JavaScript can dynamically change CSS styles of elements using the style property, enabling visual updates without reloading the page.

JavaScript.title: Forms
JavaScript.content: Forms collect user input, and JavaScript can validate or process this data before sending it to a server.

JavaScript.title: Form Validation
JavaScript.content: Form validation ensures that user inputs meet certain criteria before submission, improving data accuracy and user experience.

JavaScript.title: JSON
JavaScript.content: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight format used for storing and exchanging data between a server and a client.

JavaScript.title: Local Storage
JavaScript.content: Local storage allows web applications to store data in the browser persistently, even after the page is refreshed or closed.

JavaScript.title: Session Storage
JavaScript.content: Session storage stores data temporarily and is cleared when the browser tab is closed, making it useful for short-term storage.

JavaScript.title: setTimeout
JavaScript.content: setTimeout is used to execute a function after a specified delay in milliseconds, useful for timing events.

JavaScript.title: setInterval
JavaScript.content: setInterval repeatedly executes a function at specified intervals, making it useful for animations or repeated tasks.

JavaScript.title: Debugging
JavaScript.content: Debugging is the process of identifying and fixing errors in code using tools like console logs and browser developer tools.

JavaScript.title: Arrow Functions
JavaScript.content: Arrow functions are a shorter and more modern way of writing functions in JavaScript. They provide a cleaner syntax and automatically bind the this keyword differently compared to regular functions, making them useful in many situations such as callbacks and array methods.

JavaScript.title: Template Literals
JavaScript.content: Template literals use backticks (`) and allow embedding variables and expressions directly inside strings using ${}. They make string formatting more readable and flexible.

JavaScript.title: Destructuring
JavaScript.content: Destructuring allows you to extract values from arrays or objects into separate variables in a concise and readable way, reducing repetitive code.

JavaScript.title: Spread Operator
JavaScript.content: The spread operator (...) allows you to expand elements of arrays or objects, making it easier to copy, merge, or pass values.

JavaScript.title: Rest Parameter
JavaScript.content: The rest parameter (...) allows a function to accept an indefinite number of arguments as an array, making it flexible for handling multiple inputs.

JavaScript.title: Default Parameters
JavaScript.content: Default parameters allow you to assign default values to function parameters if no argument is provided, ensuring the function still works properly.

JavaScript.title: Promises
JavaScript.content: A Promise is an object that represents the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation. It allows developers to handle async tasks more effectively than traditional callbacks.

JavaScript.title: then() Method
JavaScript.content: The .then() method is used with promises to define what should happen when the promise is successfully resolved.

JavaScript.title: catch() Method
JavaScript.content: The .catch() method handles errors that occur in a promise chain, ensuring proper error management.

JavaScript.title: finally() Method
JavaScript.content: The .finally() method executes code after a promise is settled, regardless of whether it was fulfilled or rejected.

JavaScript.title: Async/Await
JavaScript.content: Async/await is a modern way to handle asynchronous operations, making code look synchronous and easier to read and maintain.

JavaScript.title: Fetch API
JavaScript.content: The Fetch API is used to make network requests to servers and retrieve data, often in JSON format, for use in web applications.

JavaScript.title: API
JavaScript.content: An API (Application Programming Interface) allows different software systems to communicate with each other by defining rules and endpoints for data exchange.

JavaScript.title: Error Handling
JavaScript.content: Error handling in JavaScript involves managing runtime errors using constructs like try, catch, and finally to prevent crashes and ensure smooth execution.

JavaScript.title: try...catch
JavaScript.content: The try block contains code that may produce an error, while the catch block handles the error if it occurs, ensuring the program continues running.

JavaScript.title: throw Statement
JavaScript.content: The throw statement allows you to create custom errors and control how they are handled in your program.

JavaScript.title: Classes
JavaScript.content: Classes are templates for creating objects, allowing developers to define properties and methods in a structured way using object-oriented programming.

JavaScript.title: Constructor
JavaScript.content: A constructor is a special method inside a class that is automatically called when a new object is created, used to initialize properties.

JavaScript.title: Inheritance
JavaScript.content: Inheritance allows a class to inherit properties and methods from another class, promoting code reuse and organization.

JavaScript.title: Modules
JavaScript.content: Modules allow code to be split into separate files and reused across applications, improving maintainability and scalability.

JavaScript.title: Export
JavaScript.content: The export statement is used to make functions, variables, or classes available for use in other modules.

JavaScript.title: Import
JavaScript.content: The import statement allows you to use exported code from other files, enabling modular programming.

JavaScript.title: Closures
JavaScript.content: Closures occur when a function remembers variables from its outer scope even after the outer function has finished executing, allowing powerful patterns like data privacy.

JavaScript.title: Hoisting
JavaScript.content: Hoisting is JavaScript’s behavior of moving variable and function declarations to the top of their scope before code execution.

JavaScript.title: Strict Mode
JavaScript.content: Strict mode enforces stricter parsing and error handling in JavaScript, helping developers write safer and cleaner code.

JavaScript.title: Callbacks
JavaScript.content: A callback is a function passed as an argument to another function, allowing asynchronous or delayed execution.

JavaScript.title: Event Loop
JavaScript.content: The event loop is a mechanism that handles asynchronous operations in JavaScript, ensuring non-blocking execution by managing tasks and callbacks.

JavaScript.title: setImmediate / Microtasks
JavaScript.content: Meaning: Microtasks (like promises) run before macrotasks (like setTimeout), ensuring faster execution in the event loop queue system.

JavaScript.title: Web Storage API
JavaScript.content: The Web Storage API includes localStorage and sessionStorage, allowing browsers to store key-value data for web applications.

JavaScript.title: ES6
JavaScript.content: ES6 (ECMAScript 2015) introduced major improvements to JavaScript, including arrow functions, classes, modules, and more modern syntax.

JavaScript.title: DOM Traversal
JavaScript.content: DOM traversal refers to navigating through elements in the DOM tree, such as parent, child, and sibling elements, allowing developers to precisely target and manipulate specific parts of a webpage structure.

JavaScript.title: parentNode / childNodes
JavaScript.content: These properties allow access to an element’s parent or children in the DOM, enabling structured navigation and manipulation of HTML elements.

JavaScript.title: createElement
JavaScript.content: The createElement method is used to dynamically create new HTML elements using JavaScript, which can then be added to the webpage.

JavaScript.title: appendChild
JavaScript.content: The appendChild method adds a new child element to an existing element in the DOM, helping dynamically update page content.

JavaScript.title: removeChild
JavaScript.content: The removeChild method removes a child element from the DOM, allowing dynamic deletion of elements from a webpage.

JavaScript.title: replaceChild
JavaScript.content: The replaceChild method replaces one child element with another, enabling updates to existing DOM elements efficiently.

JavaScript.title: classList
JavaScript.content: The classList property allows adding, removing, or toggling CSS classes on elements, making it easier to manage styles dynamically.

JavaScript.title: addEventListener
JavaScript.content: The addEventListener method attaches an event handler to an element without overwriting existing handlers, making event handling more flexible.

JavaScript.title: removeEventListener
JavaScript.content: This method removes an event listener previously attached, helping control event behavior and prevent unwanted actions.

JavaScript.title: preventDefault
JavaScript.content: The preventDefault method stops the default behavior of an event, such as preventing form submission or link navigation.

JavaScript.title: stopPropagation
JavaScript.content: This method prevents an event from bubbling up or capturing down the DOM tree, controlling how events propagate.

JavaScript.title: Event Bubbling
JavaScript.content: Event bubbling is when an event starts from the target element and propagates upward through its ancestors in the DOM.

JavaScript.title: Event Capturing
JavaScript.content: Event capturing is the opposite of bubbling, where the event starts from the root and moves down to the target element.

JavaScript.title: Debouncing
JavaScript.content: Debouncing limits how often a function runs by delaying its execution until a certain time has passed without repeated triggering, useful for performance optimization.

JavaScript.title: Throttling
JavaScript.content: Throttling ensures a function executes at most once in a specified time interval, improving performance in events like scrolling.

JavaScript.title: Memory Management
JavaScript.content: Memory management involves handling allocation and deallocation of memory in JavaScript, ensuring efficient use and preventing memory leaks.

JavaScript.title: Garbage Collection
JavaScript.content: Garbage collection automatically removes unused objects from memory, helping optimize performance and avoid memory issues.

JavaScript.title: Performance Optimization
JavaScript.content: Meaning: Performance optimization involves improving code efficiency by reducing unnecessary operations, improving load time, and enhancing responsiveness.

JavaScript.title: Lazy Loading
JavaScript.content: Lazy loading delays the loading of resources until they are needed, improving initial page load speed and performance.

JavaScript.title: Web Workers
JavaScript.content: Web Workers allow JavaScript to run scripts in background threads, preventing blocking of the main UI thread.

JavaScript.title: Security (XSS)
JavaScript.content: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) is a security vulnerability where attackers inject malicious scripts into web pages viewed by others, which JavaScript must guard against.

JavaScript.title: CORS
JavaScript.content: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is a security feature that controls how resources are requested from different domains.

JavaScript.title: Cookies
JavaScript.content: Cookies are small pieces of data stored in the browser that are sent to the server with requests, often used for sessions and tracking.

JavaScript.title: Authentication
JavaScript.content: Authentication verifies the identity of a user, ensuring that only authorized individuals can access certain features or data.

JavaScript.title: Authorization
JavaScript.content: Authorization determines what an authenticated user is allowed to do, controlling access to resources and actions.

JavaScript.title: Testing
JavaScript.content: Testing ensures that JavaScript code works correctly by checking for bugs and validating expected behavior using tools and frameworks.

JavaScript.title: Unit Testing
JavaScript.content: Unit testing focuses on testing individual components or functions to ensure each part of the code works as expected.

JavaScript.title: Integration Testing
JavaScript.content: Integration testing checks how different parts of a system work together, ensuring proper interaction between modules.

JavaScript.title: Frameworks
JavaScript.content: Frameworks like React, Angular, and Vue provide pre-built structures and tools to simplify building complex web applications.

JavaScript.title: Libraries
JavaScript.content: Libraries are collections of reusable code that developers can use to perform common tasks more efficiently without writing everything from scratch.
